steps.action: Trepak (Russian: трeпак) or tropak (Ukrainian: т with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.
steps.text: Trepak (Russian: трeпак) or tropak (Ukrainian: т with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.

steps.action: The dance is included in the UNESCO Intangible C
steps.text: The dance is included in the UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage List of the Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region, whose form is preserved in Tacheng.

steps.action: After a few seconds, another dancer would come o
steps.text: After a few seconds, another dancer would come out to meet him and take the same pose.

steps.action: The other dancer spun around in place, threw him
steps.text: The other dancer spun around in place, threw himself to his knees, rushed around in a squat, crawled, twisted, and kicked his legs in different directions.

steps.action: All this was performed with enthusiasm, passion,
steps.text: All this was performed with enthusiasm, passion, and complete dedication.

steps.action: " The characteristic element is a simple walk wi
steps.text: " The characteristic element is a simple walk with a syncopated stamp, often done to a quick duple meter rhythm.

steps.action: On So You Think You Can Dance (Season 4), Joshua
steps.text: On So You Think You Can Dance (Season 4), Joshua Allen and Stephen "Twitch" Boss performed a Trepak routine, interpreted as a dance duel, in Week 9 (August 6, 2008).

steps.text: The third of Anton Rubinstein's Seven National Dances, Op.

steps.action: The third of Modest Mussorgsky's Songs and Dance
steps.text: The third of Modest Mussorgsky's Songs and Dances of Death (mid-1870s) is named "Trepak".

steps.action: The dance was also used in the last movement of
steps.text: The dance was also used in the last movement of Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky's Violin Concerto in D major, Op.

steps.action: One of its best known representations is "Trepak
steps.text: One of its best known representations is "Trepak" (also known as the "Russian Dance") from Tchaikovsky's ballet The Nutcracker (1892).

steps.action: It is featured in the “Dance and Song of the Sko
steps.text: It is featured in the “Dance and Song of the Skomorokhs” from the first scene of Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov's opera-bylina “Sadko” (1897).

steps.action: The "Russian dance" in Igor Stravinsky's ballet
steps.text: The "Russian dance" in Igor Stravinsky's ballet Petrushka (1911) features a Trepak.

steps.action: An arrangement of a Ukrainian folk variant for o
steps.text: An arrangement of a Ukrainian folk variant for orchestra and soprano titled "Tropak" by Louis Katzman has been recorded in 1928 at Brunswick records in the USA.

steps.text: The Russian researcher Larisa Timoshenko describes the process as follows: "The Trepak usually began with an “entrance.

steps.action: One stood in place, performing various knee move
steps.text: One stood in place, performing various knee movements with his feet, moving his shoulders and the entire upper part of his torso.

steps.action: Accompaniment is usually on two alternating chor
steps.text: Accompaniment is usually on two alternating chords; dominant and tonic.

steps.action: 82 (1868) is named "Russkaya I Trepak" and is sp
steps.text: 82 (1868) is named "Russkaya I Trepak" and is split into two movements.
